title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma v. Hindustan Polymers Ltd. & Anr.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was effected without following the principles of natural justice as no show cause notice was issued prior to the dismissal order dated 14th March 2022.

The domestic enquiry conducted by the respondent company was vitiated as the Enquiry Officer was not an independent authority but a senior official directly subordinate to the General Manager who had initiated the disciplinary proceedings.

The alleged misconduct of causing financial loss was not substantiated by any documentary evidence on record and the charge sheet was vague and did not specify the exact nature of the acts complained of.
The petitioner had completed 17 years of continuous service with an unblemished record and the punishment of dismissal was disproportionate even assuming the charges were proved.

The respondent failed to comply with Section 33 of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 by not obtaining prior permission from the Conciliation Officer before dismissing the petitioner during the pendency of conciliation proceedings.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Delhi allowed the writ petition filed by the petitioner-employee challenging his dismissal from service. The Court held that the domestic enquiry was fundamentally flawed due to the lack of independence of the Enquiry Officer and the failure to issue a proper show cause notice. The termination order was quashed and the respondent was directed to reinstate the petitioner with full back wages and continuity of service within sixty days.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
W.P.(C) No. 11482 of 2022

JUDGMENT
Reserved on: 08.08.2023
Pronounced on: 22.09.2023

HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE PRADEEP KUMAR SINHA

1. The present writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India has been filed by the petitioner, Ramesh Kumar Sharma, challenging the order of dismissal dated 14th March 2022 passed by Respondent No. 1, Hindustan Polymers Ltd., and seeking reinstatement with full back wages and continuity of service.

2. Brief facts giving rise to the present petition are that the petitioner was employed as a Senior Accounts Executive with Respondent No. 1 since 3rd January 2005. A charge sheet was issued on 19th November 2021 alleging that the petitioner had caused financial irregularities to the tune of Rs. 4,87,000 during the financial year 2020-21. A domestic enquiry was conducted and upon receipt of the Enquiry Officer's report finding the petitioner guilty, the dismissal order was passed.

3. Learned counsel for the petitioner, Mr. Aditya Rao, submitted that the entire disciplinary proceedings are void ab initio as the Enquiry Officer, one Shri V.K. Mehra, was a Deputy General Manager directly working under the General Manager, Shri Harpal Singh, who had himself issued the charge sheet. It was contended that this arrangement violated the fundamental requirement of an independent adjudicator as laid down by the Supreme Court in Punjab National Bank v. Kunj Behari Misra, (1998) 7 SCC 84.

4. It was further argued that no show cause notice on penalty was issued to the petitioner before passing the dismissal order, thereby violating the twin requirements of natural justice. The petitioner was never given an opportunity to show cause against the proposed punishment, which is a mandatory requirement under the Standing Orders applicable to the establishment.

5. Per contra, learned counsel for the respondent, Ms. Sunita Grover, submitted that the enquiry was conducted fairly, the petitioner participated in the proceedings, and the findings of fact arrived at by the Enquiry Officer are not amenable to judicial review under Article 226 of the Constitution.

6. Having heard the learned counsels and perused the record, this Court is of the considered opinion that the domestic enquiry suffers from a fundamental jurisdictional infirmity. The appointment of Shri V.K. Mehra as Enquiry Officer, who was directly subordinate to the very officer who initiated the disciplinary action, creates a reasonable apprehension of bias. The principle that justice must not only be done but must manifestly be seen to be done is well established. The arrangement in the present case fails this test.

7. Furthermore, this Court finds that no show cause notice on the quantum of punishment was ever issued to the petitioner. The Standing Orders of the company, which have statutory force, specifically mandate such a notice. Non-compliance with this requirement renders the dismissal order procedurally infirm and unsustainable in law.

8. This Court also notes that the petitioner had a service record of seventeen years without any previous disciplinary action. Even assuming the charges were established, the punishment of dismissal would be grossly disproportionate to the alleged misconduct, particularly in the absence of any direct pecuniary gain to the petitioner.

9. In view of the foregoing, the impugned dismissal order dated 14th March 2022 is hereby quashed and set aside. Respondent No. 1 is directed to reinstate the petitioner in service with full back wages, all consequential service benefits, and continuity of service within a period of sixty days from the date of this judgment. The respondent shall be at liberty to conduct a fresh enquiry in accordance with law, if so advised, ensuring compliance with the principles of natural justice.

10. The writ petition is allowed accordingly with costs of Rs. 25,000 to be paid by Respondent No. 1 to the petitioner.

(PRADEEP KUMAR SINHA)
JUDGE
SEPTEMBER 22, 2023
